macro_segments.title: The Principle of Hazakah in Testimony about Brothers and Sons: Why the Husband is Believed to Release from Yibbum but Not to Bind

micro_segments.title: Setting up the problem: The Mishnah's rule that the husband is believed to free the wife from yibbum but not to bind her
Abaye's explanation: The husband is believed only to reinforce the existing presumption (hazakah) that she is not obligated in yibbum
The Baraita of Rabbi Natan: When the husband said 'no brothers' at betrothal but retracted on his deathbed, he is believed to bind her in yibbum
The logic of mah li leshaker: Why the husband would not lie at betrothal (he could have simply given her a get), making his statement equivalent to witnesses
Rabbi's view: The first mah li leshaker completely uprooted the hazakah, so now she has a presumption of being free and he cannot bind her on his deathbed statement
Rabbi Natan's view: The first mah li leshaker only neutralized the original hazakah, not completely erased it, so his deathbed statement reinforcing it is believed
Summary: The Gemara shows that even Rabbi Natan agrees the husband cannot make a free wife forbidden; he is only believed when reinforcing a pre-existing hazakah

macro_segments.title: Marrying Off a Daughter Without Specifying Which One: Multiple Daughters, Groups, and the Limits of Ambiguity

micro_segments.title: The case of bogeret daughters: A father who married off his daughter without specifying which one; bogeret daughters are excluded because he has no authority to marry them off
Gemara's clarification: The plural 'bogrot' does not mean he has multiple adult daughters; it means in cases like this, people do not refer to adult daughters
The chitush: When the adult daughter made him her shaliach (agent), he is then empowered to marry off both adult and underage daughters, but we still presume he means the underage one
The reason: The father has more responsibility and urgency to marry off underage daughters; they are under his control and must transition from his house to a husband's house
Two groups of daughters: When a father has two sets of daughters (from two different wives), and says he married off 'the oldest,' the ambiguity multiplies
Rabbi Meir's view: A person is willing to enter ambiguity; all daughters who could be called 'oldest' might be married, except the very youngest of the youngest group
Rabbi Yosi's view: A person does not create ambiguity for themselves; 'oldest' means only the most specific and unambiguous oldest of the oldest group
The Pesach parallel: The Gemara compares the debate to the case of 'until the face of Pesach' and notes that initially it seems the positions are reversed
Abaye's precise formulation: The debate only applies when there are two distinct groups; if all daughters are in one group, 'oldest' means only the truly oldest
Challenge and response: Rav Adda bar Masna asks what if there are three daughters in the youngest group; Abaye clarifies the Mishnah presumes only two in each group
Resolution: The positions are actually reversed in the Pesach case because there the debate is about the meaning of the word 'face,' not about whether people enter ambiguity

macro_segments.title: Disputed Marriage Claims: When Each Party Says Opposite Things About Whether Kiddushin Occurred

micro_segments.title: He says 'I married you,' she says 'No, you didn't': He is bound by his own statement (cannot marry her relatives), but she is not bound and can marry anyone
She says 'You married me,' he says 'No, I didn't': She is bound by her statement (cannot marry his relatives), but he is not bound in the same way
He says 'I married you,' she says 'No, you married my daughter': Each is bound only by their own claim; neither binds the other person
He says 'I married your daughter,' she says 'No, you married me': Each is bound only by their own statement

Gemara's reasoning: A man's self-binding claim costs him little, so we don't bind the woman based on it; a woman's claim costs her greatly (all other marriages forbidden), so it is inherently credible
Mother's claim about her daughter does not bind the daughter: The Torah gives the father power to marry off an underage daughter, not the mother; the Gemara asks why not apply this rabbinically
When the woman claims she is married and the man denies it: Shmuel says we ask (request) him to give a get; Rav says if he gives it on his own, we force him to pay ketubah
The interplay between request and coercion: Shmuel says request a get; Rav adds that if he gives it on his own initiative, it is an admission and we can force the ketubah payment

macro_segments.title: The Requirement for Witnesses in Kiddushin: Are Witnesses Necessary to Prove it Happened or to Make it Legally Binding?

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: 'One witness is not enough for kiddushin' — this is stated as a general rule but its precise meaning is unclear
The question: Does 'two witnesses required' mean they must be present to make it binding, or only to prove it occurred? Test case: both parties agree and one witness testifies—is it binding?
Rav Yehuda's uncertainty: He could not clearly answer whether the need for two witnesses is to establish binding effect or merely to prove the fact
Shmuel's position (cited by Rav Nachman): Even if both parties agree, without two witnesses present the kiddushin is not binding
Rava's challenge from the Mishnah: The Mishnah discusses claims ('he said / she said') without mentioning witnesses, suggesting kiddushin does not require witnesses to be present
Gemara's resolution: The Mishnah presumes cases where at least one witness was present but is not currently testifying; he claims there were witnesses present at the time
Implication: All the previous Mishnahs discussing who to believe about marriage must be understood as claims that witnesses were present, even if not currently produced
The Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel debate on remarriage after divorce: Whether witnesses seeing them in seclusion together counts as testimony of sexual union that creates new kiddushin
Clarification of the debate: It is not about whether witnesses are required, but about what the witnesses have to see and what we presume happened
Beis Shammai: Witnesses seeing seclusion is not the same as witnessing sexual union; without prior history, seclusion does not create kiddushin
Beis Hillel: If they were previously married and had sex, witnesses seeing them in seclusion is treated as equivalent to witnessing sexual union, creating new kiddushin
Both agree: If they never had sexual relations before, seclusion alone is not treated as evidence of sexual union, so no kiddushin occurs

topical_tags.term: Mah li leshaker (why would he lie)
Kiddushin she-einam mesurim l'viah (kiddushin that cannot culminate in intercourse)
Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yosi on oldest/youngest
Ne'eman lehatir ve-eino ne'eman le-asor (believed to free, not to bind)
Beis Shammai vs. Beis Hillel on remarriage
